paper.title: The Impact of Bisphenol A on Thyroid Function in Neonates and Children: A Systematic Review of the Literature

Division of Endocrinology and Metabolism, Center of Clinical, Experimental Surgery and Translational Research, Biomedical Research Foundation of the Academy of Athens, 11527 Athens, Greece
The Impact of Bisphenol A on Thyroid Function in Neonates and Children: A Systematic Review of the Literature

evidence_entities.text: The researchers revealed a statistically significant (p = 0.01) inverse relationship in male neonates (−9.9% per log2 unit; 95% CI: −15.9%, −3.5%) but not in females (4.4% for every doubling in average BPA; 95% CI: −2.4%, 11.7).
A 10-fold increase in mean BPA was associated with lower cord TSH concentrations in girls (percent change = −36.0%; 95%(CI): −58.4, −1.7%)

A significant inverse relationship between BPA and TSH concentrations in both boys and girls was noted (βhighest = −1.91, 95%CI: −3.32, −0.50; for boys, βhighest = −1.89, 95%CI: −3.70, −0.50; for girls, βmiddle = −2.34, 95%CI: −4.61, −0.07).
Urinary BPA concentration was negatively associated with thyroid volume (assessed by thyroid ultrasonography) in 718 children aged 9–11 years (β = −0.033, 95% CI: −0.053, −0.013)
TSH stimulates hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the thyroid follicular cells, leading to the enlargement of the thyroid

The majority of in vitro studies suggest that BPA binds to thyroid hormone receptors (TR)-alpha and TR-beta, thereby antagonizing the action of triiodothyronine (T3) and suppressing its transcriptional activity
